era_context: Late Bronze Age in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. The 18th Dynasty of Egypt is in the reign of Amenhotep II; the Hittite Empire is consolidating imperial power; Mycenaean Greece is in Late Helladic II; the Shang Dynasty rules China. This era is characterized by the development of complex diplomatic and trade networks, the use of cuneiform and hieroglyphic writing systems, and the emergence of alphabetic scripts in the Levant.

events.title: Amenhotep II's reign (approximate period, mid-18th Dynasty)
events.description: Amenhotep II (Aakheperenre) ruled Egypt during the mid-18th Dynasty, a period of imperial consolidation and military activity in the Levant. His reign is attested by temple inscriptions, administrative records, and later king lists. He is depicted as a warrior-pharaoh and hunter, though the specific chronology of his reign remains debated. His reign represents the continuation of the imperial system established by Thutmose III.

sources.name: Temple inscriptions at Karnak, Luxor, and Amada

events.certainty_note: Amenhotep II's reign is dated to approximately 1427–1400 BCE in the 'middle chronology,' but the 'low chronology' places him a century earlier, and the 'high chronology' places him later. The assignment to 1556 BCE is speculative and depends on which chronological system is adopted. Egyptian chronology for the 18th Dynasty remains one of the most contested issues in ancient history.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Amenhotep II is highly contested. Some scholars argue for a 'low chronology' that would place his reign in the 1520s–1490s BCE, while others advocate for a 'high chronology' placing him in the 1450s–1420s BCE. The 'middle chronology' (which places him c. 1427–1400 BCE) is currently the most widely accepted, but this consensus is not universal. Recent radiocarbon dating of organic materials from Egyptian tombs has suggested that some traditional dates may be off by 50–100 years, though the implications for Amenhotep II's reign specifically remain unclear. Additionally, some scholars question whether Amenhotep II was as militarily active as traditional inscriptions suggest, arguing that later pharaohs may have appropriated or exaggerated his achievements.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1556 BCE is almost entirely dependent on Egyptian temple inscriptions and king lists, both of which are elite-authored and propagandistic. No contemporary records from other civilizations (Hittites, Levantine city-states, Mycenaean Greeks) document events of this specific year. The chronological framework itself is contested and based on a combination of king list lengths, astronomical observations (heliacal risings of Sirius), and radiocarbon dating—all of which have significant margins of error. The absence of year-specific documentation means that any narrative for 1556 BCE is speculative. The historical record is heavily skewed toward elite male actors and military events; economic, social, and cultural life is underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Both entries refer to the same reign of Amenhotep II
